narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 51% chance that you would not pass—tipping just over the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.30, LSAT score of 24, Year 1 and Year 3 decile rankings in your school, family income in the middle bracket, and your plan to attend part-time. Among students who passed the bar, the average LSAT is much higher at 37.27, undergraduate GPAs average 3.24, Year 1 deciles are around 6th, and Year 3 around 6th—your profile aligns closely with successful students on some fronts but stands out on others. The three biggest factors shaping this prediction were your LSAT score, undergraduate GPA, and Year 3 decile ranking. Your LSAT score had the strongest pull toward predicting bar failure, as it falls well below what the model sees in those who succeed, signaling potential challenges in the rigorous reasoning demands of law school and the bar. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA pushed strongly in the opposite direction, toward predicting success, since your 3.30 exceeds the average among bar passers and reflects solid prior academic strength. Similarly, your Year 3 decile of 4 worked in your favor, nudging the prediction toward passing because it shows improvement and competitiveness relative to peers who went on to succeed. While your middle family income aligns with over a third of bar passers and your part-time status matches a smaller but viable group, they played lesser roles here compared to those top academic signals. This close 51% call means small shifts could change outcomes—imagine how bolstering your LSAT prep or highlighting any upward grade trends might reshape the picture. Next, consider retaking the LSAT to aim closer to 37 or above, gathering stronger letters that emphasize your resilience as a part-time student from a middle-income background, or pursuing targeted bar prep experience to demonstrate readiness. Many applicants in similar spots strengthen their profiles this way and reapply successfully—your solid GPA and later decile give you a real foundation to build on. Keep pushing; admissions models like this one evolve with new evidence of your potential.
